channel.title: Terms on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Terms on emsenn.net

item.title: Bekenstein-Hawking Entropy
item.description:
The Bekenstein-Hawking entropy is the entropy of a black hole, proportional to the area of its event horizon:

SBH=kBc3A4Gℏ=kBA4lP2S_{BH} = \frac{k_B c^3 A}{4 G \hbar} = \frac{k_B A}{4 l_P^2}

where AA is the horizon area, kBk_B is Boltzmann’s constant, GG is Newton’s gravitational constant, ℏ\hbar is the reduced Planck constant, cc is the speed of light, and lP=Gℏ/c3l_P = \sqrt{G\hbar/c^3} is the Planck length.

Jacob Bekenstein proposed in 1972-1973 that black holes must carry entropy, reasoning as follows: if a black hole had zero entropy, you could reduce the entropy of the universe by throwing high-entropy matter into it, violating the second law of thermodynamics. Bekenstein conjectured that the entropy is proportional to the horizon area. Hawking’s calculation of Hawking radiation in 1974 confirmed the proportionality and fixed the constant.

item.title: Black Hole
item.description:
A black hole is a region of spacetime enclosed by an event horizon, from which no signal – not even light – can escape to an outside observer.

Black holes are predicted by general relativity. Karl Schwarzschild found the first exact solution to Einstein’s field equations in 1916, describing the spacetime geometry around a spherically symmetric, non-rotating mass. The solution has a critical radius – the Schwarzschild radius – below which nothing can escape. For many decades, black holes were treated as mathematical curiosities. The 1960s and 1970s brought theoretical work by Penrose, Hawking, and others establishing that black holes are physical predictions of gravitational collapse.

item.title: Black Hole Information Paradox
item.description:
The black hole information paradox is the unresolved conflict between quantum mechanics – which requires that information is conserved (unitarity) – and the apparent destruction of information when a black hole evaporates via Hawking radiation.

The paradox in detail: quantum mechanics is unitary, meaning the evolution of a quantum state is reversible in principle. Given the final state, you could in principle reconstruct the initial state. But Hawking radiation appears to be exactly thermal – random noise characterized only by the black hole’s mass, charge, and angular momentum. It carries no information about the specific matter that fell into the black hole. If the black hole evaporates completely, the information about its contents appears to be destroyed, violating unitarity.

item.title: Event Horizon
item.description:
The event horizon is the boundary of a black hole – the surface beyond which events cannot affect an outside observer. It is defined by the requirement that outgoing light rays at the horizon remain trapped: they can neither escape nor fall inward, but hover at the boundary forever.

The event horizon is not a physical surface. There is no wall, no barrier, no material boundary. An observer falling through it would notice nothing locally remarkable – no sign, no sensation, no discontinuity. This is a consequence of the equivalence principle: locally, spacetime looks flat everywhere, including at the horizon. The horizon’s significance is global, not local. Once you cross it, no path through spacetime leads back out, because all future-directed paths inside the horizon lead to the singularity.

item.title: Hawking Radiation
item.description:
Hawking radiation is thermal radiation predicted to be emitted by black holes due to quantum effects near the event horizon. Stephen Hawking derived this result in 1974-1975, showing that black holes are not perfectly black.

The mechanism involves quantum field theory in curved spacetime. In the vacuum near the horizon, virtual particle-antiparticle pairs are constantly created and annihilated. Near the horizon, it is possible for one particle to fall in while the other escapes to infinity. The escaping particles constitute a thermal flux with a characteristic temperature:

item.title: Holographic Principle
item.description:
The holographic principle is the conjecture that the information content of a region of space can be fully encoded on its boundary, at a density of at most one bit per Planck area (lP2≈2.6×10−70l_P^2 \approx 2.6 \times 10^{-70} m2^2).

The principle originates from black hole entropy. The Bekenstein-Hawking entropy – the maximum entropy a region can contain before collapsing into a black hole – is proportional to the boundary area, not the volume. Gerard ’t Hooft (1993) and Leonard Susskind (1995) elevated this observation into a general principle: the fundamental degrees of freedom of any region of space reside on its boundary.

item.title: Schwarzschild Radius
item.description:
The Schwarzschild radius is the radius of the event horizon of a non-rotating, uncharged black hole:

rs=2GMc2r_s = \frac{2GM}{c^2}

where MM is the mass, GG is Newton’s gravitational constant, and cc is the speed of light.

Karl Schwarzschild derived this in 1916 as part of the first exact solution to Einstein’s field equations. The formula says the horizon radius is proportional to mass. For the Sun, rs≈3r_s \approx 3 kilometers. For the Earth, rs≈9r_s \approx 9 millimeters. For the supermassive black hole at the center of the Milky Way (~4 million solar masses), rs≈12r_s \approx 12 million kilometers.

item.title: Singularity
item.description:
A singularity is a point or region where general relativity predicts infinite spacetime curvature and infinite density. It appears at the center of a black hole and at the origin of the Big Bang.

The Penrose-Hawking singularity theorems (1960s-1970s) proved that singularities are not artifacts of special symmetry assumptions but are generic predictions of general relativity under physically reasonable conditions. If enough mass is concentrated in a small enough region, gravitational collapse to a singularity is inevitable.
